item.title: Re: BIG NARF
item.content:encoded:
Original link dead; available via archive.org

https://web.archive.org/web/20160709192129/http://drgears-site-19.tumblr.com/post/147014813338/shnoogielord-space-mist-xwingluke-last

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really embiggened my narf. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how the containment procedures for this specific object has yet to be decided on, just shows that the Foundation even if they contain about 3000 SCPs each weird in their own way they still can be got 'off guard' per say by phenomenon with as simple functions(not an insult) like this. I was considering to be neutral on this SCP however I think it deserves a vote due to the twist(that being the containment procedures for me) that really caught me.

Note: I am new here and just became active however I've read a few SCPs during my absence which did have the containment procedures ready and that's why I enjoyed this but I wont roll out the possibility of there being more SCPs like this and I just haven't found them yet.

+1 Vote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While being unable to schedule meetings and phone-conferences would be inconvenient, it seems like that the "NARF Herders" would be able to continue working with each other via email and having scheduled "free time" where they'd be free to call each other on the phone. Also, they members could be collected together into the same office so they could easily talk to each other and organized unscheduled meetings. Of course, it might be that they'd be afraid if the tried this that the effect would spread to the running of scheduled computer programs, to scheduled building maintenance, and so on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Gee, █████, what are we gonna do tonight?"
"The same thing we do every night, █████: [REDACTED]"

Just take my +1, okay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Whoops! Didn't realize the Foundation was interested in my trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel a mysterious compulsion to enjoy this one… I can't possibly think of why.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The writing's crisp as expected from Kate, but having recently binged Borges' ouvre, I'm slightly less than impressed with the ending, with BN turning into a zahir.

May or may not end up upboatin' anyways.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What Mann said. Geographic region that includes the US states of Washington and Oregon, and the Canadian province of British Columbia.

Tis a fine place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pacific Northwest.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one a lot. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PNW?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good, although I do wish there were a bit more closure. I like that a Melee tourney is considered a cultural event and a slam poetry reading isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a fair point!! I've attended slams, but only with past partners. I'll change it to "poets"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No one ever signs up with their poem title.

No one non-anomalous, I presume?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is such a nitpick, and proves how much of a nerd I am, but:

A slam poetry event in Corvallis, Oregon, United States was evacuated due to fire in the café hosting the event. “BIG NARF” was listed on the Facebook event as one of the poems to be performed.

Poetry slam events don't list the poems that will be performed, but they do publicize the poets performing (typically called the "featured" poet or the "feature"). They also frequently have open mic sign-up lists (where people put their names, not their poem titles) or a slam sign-up list (where people, again, put their names only in order to compete). No one ever signs up with their poem title.1

Footnotes
1. Source: I have been doing poetry at slams for over a decade in dozens of states all across the US.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy shit, you actually did this.

I guess it's pretty appropriate that it's a a meme within a meme within a meme.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Big Narf" was so terrible that after discussing it with other staff, we have decided to ban your IP address from the site forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, Kate comes back!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is horrible.

I hate that this dumb joke exists.

But the execution is really good.

+1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Poit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We don't know why or how it happens, we just know that it does happen (and what happens when it does).

I like that .

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Always" as in "the timespan in which this post was made to now", AKA "not literally forever", which makes it a figure of speech. Not too big of a deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Four months still seems to be pretty fresh to have been "always" or an "old abandoned idea".

Was the dream inspired by something that is related? Google just gives me the Native American Rights Foundation, and obviously there's Pinky's catchphrase, but neither seem to be directly related.

Or are we just that close to the singularity now that four months is a lifetime? :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep. Original post was made four months ago. It's here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure the tumblr post has been going around for a bit? I remember reblogging it at least a month ago.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice, but then, I would expect no less from the author. Gods, people like Kate returning strong after such a time make me ponder trying to dig some of my old abandoned ideas out of the pits, for example that article about BIG NARF…
…
I guess they'll stay abandoned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kate McTiriss! Return of a Living Legend!

This is fun, well done, fun, and… er… extremely well done!

Just one nitpick: after seeing the illustration (Calcutta Darlings), it is a bit difficult to believe that the mention Big Narf appears on any handwritten thing, where there shouldn't be any room for for it. Not sure how you can change this; if the mention Big Narf moves the handwriting around, you can no longer have the Foundation think it might not be anomalous.

But I really like the skip anyway, especially the end, that makes it tumble from 'ha ha, kids' prank' into 'Uh oh. Not good!'

Enthusiastic +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always found that joke funny

I thought this was from Gears' blog from like a couple of weeks (well 13 days) ago?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As someone who has based a lot of their material in the PNW, this skip has hit a special place in my heart. Its simplicity is as endearing as it is funny.

+1. Tis a good skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really happy that Big Narf is actually being used for a good SCP. I always found that joke funny, and this hits the right amounts of strangeness and shit to work. I also really like the scp's kind of "in-progress" state, in terms of the documentation. It's fun.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Troz!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is Pinky responsible for this? He seems like that guy to screw up stuff unintentionally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So have narf wished it, so shall narf be.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. It's creepy and mysterious and funny and weird, everything I look for in an SCP.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
oh god why did you post big narf kate?

"A Super Smash Bros. Melee"

i might get suckered in by big narf. ;c

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've always been fascinated by that first initial transition from "object of interest" to "fully detailed containment procedures." I liked being able to explore that with a phenomena in that early chaotic stage — one the Foundation really underestimated, expecting it to be an SCCP-2930-style safe linguistic weirdness

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is so goddamn silly!
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh thank god, I thought this sounded familiar!

First of all, welcome back.

Second of all, I really love articles that take place at some point other than "this thing is contained and we have all the documentation written up". This is great.

Third of all, what if big narf was bees

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: marked for deletion at -21 upvotes and then downvoted an additional 379 times

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the worst thing ever.

:)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let us go then, you and I, where the narf is spread out against the sky…

+1

see I can quote Eliot too

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So this is how the world ends, not with a big, but with a narf.
+1

item.title: BIG NARF
item.content:encoded: This very silly tumblr joke would not leave my head. I was paralyzed by it. And now, so is the Foundation in the PNW. Images by myself.
